prompt: Search X (2025-2026) for AI startup founders/engineers saying enterprise SECURITY REVIEWS, vendor security questionnaires, or SOC2 requirements are BLOCKING or DELAYING their deals. Quote specific posts verbatim with handles + dates. How acute and how common is this pain? Cite.
content:
**This pain point is discussed recurrently on X in 2025–2026, particularly around SOC 2 as "table stakes" (or theater) for enterprise sales, security questionnaires/reviews causing delays or risks to deals/pilots, and related friction for startups (including AI ones).** It appears common in startup/enterprise SaaS and AI contexts, with multiple posts framing it as a blocker, source of risk, or major slowdown—sometimes with high engagement (hundreds to thousands of likes on key threads). It is not universally "blocking all deals" but is portrayed as acute enough that founders often delay compliance until a deal is at risk, and enterprise AI projects frequently stall or "die" in review.[[1]](https://x.com/security_pal/status/2023687623617740891)[[2]](https://x.com/agenticafy/status/2033619314448732421)

Direct verbatim quotes from relevant posts (focusing on 2025–2026, with handles and dates; limited exact matches from self-identified "AI startup founders/engineers" but strong thematic overlap with AI/enterprise sales discussions):

- **@OfficialCKhoury (Christian Khoury), June 2, 2026**: ""We'll get to it when there's a big deal on the table." Most founders wait until a prospect asks for the SOC 2 report before starting. By then, the deal is already at risk."[[3]](https://x.com/OfficialCKhoury/status/2061832773552484780)

- **@haridigresses (hari raghavan), March 20, 2026** (long rant on SOC-2/InfoSec in context of Delve scandal; 470 likes): "SOC-2 startups are (at least in part) culpable for this mess, thanks to Jevon's Paradox. It's now 'easier' to get it, so getting the certification is table stakes for an enterprise contract. 'But Hari, startups can now sell to enterprise more easily' — nope. ... SOC-2-as-table-stakes killed a more pragmatic, trust-based sales motion. All in all, the introduction of SOC-2 as an industry standard introduced *more* friction into the process..." (He contrasts pre-2017 pilots without SOC-2 and notes security reviews still required anyway.)[[4]](https://x.com/haridigresses/status/2035070060050493541)

- **@security_pal (SecurityPal AI), February 17, 2026**: "Your next big deal could be stuck behind a security questionnaire backlog."[[1]](https://x.com/security_pal/status/2023687623617740891)

- **@agenticafy (Agenticafy), March 16, 2026** (AI analytics context): "90% of enterprise AI analytics projects die in the security review."[[2]](https://x.com/agenticafy/status/2033619314448732421)

- **@twofifteenam (Telt), March 22, 2025**: "The key to being a good startup founder is delaying getting soc2. Once you get it you can’t push to prod, you can’t move fast. You’ve lost."[[5]](https://x.com/twofifteenam/status/1903323616327917895)

- **@AIxBlock (AIxBlock), May 20, 2026** (enterprise AI data context): "Your AI data vendor signed the NDA. Promised exclusivity. Passed security review. Then your compliance team asked to see the architecture diagram. That conversation is where most enterprise AI projects stall in 2026."[[6]](https://x.com/AIxBlock/status/2057139158490268133)

Supporting context from related high-engagement posts (e.g., SOC 2 friction broadly, with AI/enterprise mentions):
- **@frantzfries (Chris Frantz), August 20, 2025** (429 likes): "we need a new standard, SOC2 is cooked at this point... some startups hit checkboxes and get the same cert in a couple days + short window. zero faith as a buyer."[[7]](https://x.com/frantzfries/status/1958139673521369288)
- Multiple posts note enterprise pilots or deals "stuck in security review" and how tools/processes can "unblock" them, or that security/compliance reviews (alongside legal/risk) are now a "trust procurement race" for regulated/enterprise AI.[[8]](https://x.com/kerrsee/status/2060118422051311813)[[9]](https://x.com/AethelredHQ/status/2061798860234903752)

**Overall assessment of acuteness/commonality**: The topic surfaces repeatedly in startup founder, compliance, and AI/enterprise sales circles, often with frustration over it being performative ("security theater"), costly/time-consuming, yet mandatory for larger deals. High-engagement threads (e.g., 400–1,500+ likes) and phrases like "deal at risk," "stuck," "stall," "die in the security review," or "big deal on the table" indicate it is a *real and common pain* rather than niche—especially acute for early-stage or AI-native startups pursuing enterprise customers, where reviews act as a gate that can delay or derail momentum. Founders reportedly treat SOC 2 reactively, and AI-specific enterprise work amplifies it due to data/privacy sensitivities. Solutions discussed include automation, better proxies for security, or bypassing via pilots. No evidence of it being universally fatal, but it is consistently called out as a friction point adding cost and delay.
